auditor_report.auditor: Audit Services Corporation (Office of the Federal Auditor General)
auditor_report.opinion_type: Qualified (except for matters in Basis for Qualified Opinion)

basis_for_qualified_opinion_findings.text:
1. The Sugar Corporation Import account (01-12500-6000) exhibits a material long-
standing outstanding balance of ETB 266,000,090 dating back to the 2014 fiscal
i                    year, persisting without resolution in the subsequent financial statements of the
                        SIDF. This balance is obscured in the import financial statement that indicates a
lack of proper accounting treatment, raising concerns regarding the accuracy and
he                            completeness of the financial reporting.

2. We have identified notable deficiencies within the inventory management system
B                              of the Corporation, which demand immediate attention. Such as:
j                              = The inventory cost flow assumptions as policy is not consistent throughout the
corporation. in consequence the value of inventory reported in the statement of
«                                  financial position is the sum of inventory valued at weighted average method in
Kesem and inventory valued at first in first out method in Metehara, Wonji Shoa
and Fincha Sugar Factories. Weak inventory system has result in full provision
E                                    of inventories in Tendaho and Welkait Sugar Projects.
j                       =
ie        ‘& 251-011-5515222         Fax 251-011-5513083         E-mail asc@ascethiopia.com
251-011-5535012,                                              Website www.ascethiopia.                  or,

a                               INDEPENDENT AUDITOR’S REPORT
                          TO THE MANAGEMENT OF
ETHIOPIAN SUGAR INDUSTRY GROUP (continued)
i                             Basis for Qualified Opinion(continued)
                                         We observed the absence of financial stock cards. This resulted in discrepancies
between the physical inventory count and the ledger balance. As evidenced,
there is difference of ETB 312,428,552 between the recorded inventory and the
a                       actual inventory count the latter is more in amount. This variance has been
                                             accounted for under Cost of goods sold, plantation, and administrative expenses,
necessitating further investigation. The lack of financial stock cards not only
a                               hinders the company’s ability to effectively track inventory but also
compromises the reliability and completeness of portion allotted to Cost of
goods sold, plantation, and administrative expenses. Consequently, the
?                              adjustment of ETB 312,428,552 in the prorated balance for these accounts has
not been verified for audit purposes.
7                            "= For an industry like the sugar sector, which typically encompasses a myriad of
7                               inventory line items. We identified a concerning practice of consolidating
                       various inventory items into aggregated sums, instead of providing detailed
breakdowns, at Arjo Sugar Factory, Kessem Sugar Factory, and Tana Beles
a                                 ‘sugar projects. This practice hinders accurate inventory tracking, cost analysis,
2                                        and effective management.
7                            * For an industry like the sugar sector, which typically encompasses a myriad of
                                  inventory line items, for instance Arjo Sugar Factory has adopted a practice of
consolidating inventory items into aggregated sums. However, Arjo keeps
                               inventory items on a lump sum basis. For example, a general store items worth
4                            ETB 100,746,095 (ETB 85,210,605 in 2017) was amalgamated and accounted
for as a single line as general store item.
                            = The Corporation has consumable inventories and goods in transit worth of ETB
553,062,883 consisting long outstanding and slow-moving inventories, which
                                   have been increasing from year to year but are not measured at lower cost or net
                                  realizable value. Despite this fact, the provision for stock obsolescence showed
no movement over the last three years, overstating the value of inventory with
*                       an amount that should have been accounted as impairment on stock yearly.
                                          Moreover, an inventory account consists of items with abnormal balances which
=                                is caused by inappropriate accounting on which the management has not taken
=                                     investigative action.
i                              Consequently, we were unable to determine the completeness and accuracy of stock
& goods in transit and cost of sales shown on the statement of financial position and
jj                      statement of profit or loss and other comprehensive income at ETB 5,790,184,061
                          and ETB 9,929,563,760 respectively.
a

3. There was no system of internal control over the accounting of intercompany
4                                accounts on which we could rely for the purposes of our audit. As a result, the Inter-
company receivable and Inter-company payable accounts on the branches’ financial
statement did not match the Inter- company payable and Inter-company receivable
                           of other factories or projects. -                                      a

5                        TO THE MANAGEMENT OF
at                         ETHIOPIAN SUGAR INDUSTRY GROUP (continued)
Basis for Qualified Opinion(continued)
=                          “In the process of elimination of the intercompany receivable and payable the
                          residual figure worth of ETB 90,021,480 is debited to Sugar Industry Development
Fund account (Equity account) account without considering the economics of the
=                       transactions. Moreover, lack of internal control over the accounting of funding the
:                               Sugar Industry Development Fund account on the statement of financial position of
ya                    each branch do not agree with the balance at the Group level. In the process of
                            eliminating this account net amount of ETB 2,049,282 is debited to the Head office
account (equity account).
                       Therefore, the equity account entitled Head  office account balance of ETB
17,879,927,787 is understated with the sum of the above stated figure.
x                 4, The financial statements reflect advance payments to contractors as per Note 16,
amounting to ETB 14,872,745,556 with ETB 7,971,912,333 (54%) being impaired.
—                            This indicates a deficiency in the internal control system governing the tracking and
recovery of advance payments. Given that these funds represent public money and
q                                  remain unsettled, we would typically anticipate legal actions or appropriate measures
—                            to address these outstanding advances.
a

04.
Therefore, due to the lack of a robust internal control framework, we do not have
=                confidence in relying on the total Cost of sales figure, which amounts to ETB
S                       8,573,028,777.
=

5. We have identified notable deficiencies within the cost accounting system of the
x!                          corporation which demand immediate attention. Such as:
a                        = The absence of a cost accounting system resulted in the cost of sugarcane in in
                               ‘Arjo Sugar factory, amounting to ETB 87,038,034 (1,110,462 tons), being
                            calculated based on the proportion of harvested sugar to the total agricultural
                         area, without accounting for depreciation, This approach lacked the necessary
controls to ensure proper accounting for inventory consumption and other related
E                              production costs. This lack of traceability hinders the company's ability to
                              accurately track inventory usage and associated costs, potentially impacting the
j                             reliability of production cost calculations.
a                            = It is difficult to determine the costs associated with producing 193,630 Quintals
for the year in Kessem sugar Corporation. Expenses, inclusive of the cost of sales,
nu                       have been aggregated under the general and administrative expense category.
4                                   For instance, other expenses consisting of various product costs amounting ETB
                             175,732,596 that should have been classified and allotted as overhead cost and
incorporated in cost of inventory, are accounted as expenses totally. Moreover,
ia                                 we were unable to adequately relate the amount of issued inventory to the sugar
                                  cane field where it was used and to the associated production costs. This lack of
traceability hinders the company's ability to accurately track inventory usage and
Si                              associated costs, potentially impacting the reliability of production cost
 y                              calculations.                                                    wee
i      ase         LN
5              (be);
z
  :
   4
INDEPENDENT AUDITOR’S REPORT
=                        TO THE MANAGEMENT OF
C4                            ETHIOPIAN SUGAR INDUSTRY GROUP (continued)
Basis for Qualified Opinion(continued)
—                     = The absence of a cost accounting system resulted in the cost of sugarcane in
OMO II Sugar factory. For instance, we were unable to adequately relate the
                                    amount of inventory issued to the sugar cane fields, where it was used and to the
a                     associated production costs. This lack of traceability hinders the company's
:                     ability to accurately track inventory usage and associated costs, potentially
                                 impacting the reliability of production cost calculations. Consequently, we were
-                           unable to delineate the costs linked with the production of 62,155 Quintals for
the year and inhibit our confidence in relying on the total cost of goods sold
                                     figure, amounting to ETB 318,683,980.
= The absence of a cost accounting system resulted in the cost of sugarcane in
=                           OMO III Sugar factory. For instance, we were unable to adequately relate the
amount of issued inventory to the sugar cane field where it was used and to the
a                            associated production costs. In addition, in the fair value determination process
                        the factory utilizes a constant value per hectare amount ETB 35,296.81 since
2019 fiscal year. This lack of traceability hinders the company's ability to
7                               accurately track inventory usage and associated costs, potentially impacting the
-—                    reliability of production cost calculations. Consequently, we were unable to
delineate the costs linked with the production of $2,530 Quintals for the year and
7                 inhibits our confidence in relying on the total cost of goods sold. figure,
=)                                amounting to ETB 237,694,8

6. The property, plant and equipment consisting of CIP-tissue culture for an amount of
ETB 74,185,564 has exhibited no movement over a significant period and has not
Pe                      been capitalized. This indicates a potential lack of asset utilization by the
=                                Corporation. As a result, we were unable to ascertain whether adjustments have been
found necessary in respect of property, plant and equipment shown on the statement
i                               of financial position at ETB 142,831,696,435.

7. Included in trade and other receivables is receivable from Commercial Bank of
Ethiopia amounting to ETB 71,464,864 (70,540,004 in 2019). We have obtained no
&                     sufficient and appropriate audit evidence that this balance is recoverable. In
consequence we were unable to determine whether any adjustment might have been
found necessary in respect of Trade and other receivables shown on the statement of
ie                             financial position at ETB 1,754,168,000.

8. Despite the fact that a provision of ETB 400,630,800 was held in advance and
fe                   prepayment accounts of Wonji Sugar Factory for the out growers’, a substantial
ry                             portion of this provision was allocated for the sugar cane plantation damaged during
political unrest in 2010 E.C. and for some farms that unilaterally terminated their
a                       contracts with the Corporation. Given that these funds represent public money and
]                                  remain unsettled, we would typically anticipate legal actions or appropriate measures
to address these outstanding advances.                               ——

3          Basis for Qualified Opinion(continued)

9. We identified significant deficiencies in the internal control over cash and cash
                      equivalents in Arjo Sugar factory. These weaknesses limit our ability to form an
a                            opinion on the reliability of the reported cash and cash equivalents balance of ETB
:                            21,805,757 in the factory.
s

10. The revenue shown in the statement of profit or loss & other comprehensive income
of ETB 9,929,563,760 is the cumulative total of the revenue recorded in the separate
=)                            records of the head office and the sugar factories when the produce is put under the
control of the head office and is measured at transfer prices determined by the Board
7                 of Directors of the Corporation. This constitutes a departure from IFRS which
2                                 requires that a sale be recognized at the transaction price on delivery to the customer.
In consequence we were unable to satisfy ourselves that the revenue shown in the
"1                            statement of profit or loss and other comprehensive income is correctly stated.
x

11. As discussed in Note 28.6 (Capital management) of the notes to the financial
                             statements, gearing (Debt to Equity ratio) of the Corporation stood at 121% (115%
in 2020) which imply the Corporation has larger proportion of debt than equity, so
a                        would not be able to pay down its debt unless serious measures are taken in this
s                       respect.
a

12. Paid up capital of the Corporation is summation of the carrying amount of net asset
a                            of each of the existing factories amounting ETB 6,042,512,614 upon establishment
until it is increased by ETB 8,040,511,876 through in kind contribution by the
om                       government as discussed on Note 19 of the notes to the financial statements. This
contribution in kind is represented by Tendaho and Kessem sugar development
75                  dams, However, these dams were transferred to Ethiopian Construction Works
                              Corporation by Regulation no 366/2015 by the Corporation's mandate stated on the
regulation (Article 5 Sub article 3).
a)                             We conducted our audit in accordance with International Standards on Auditing (ISAs).
Our responsibilities under those standards are further described in the Auditor's
=                         Responsibilities for the Audit of the Financial Statements section of our report. We are
3                 independent of the Enterprise in accordance with the International Ethics Standards
Board for Accountants’ Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants (IESBA Code)
=                  together with the ethical requirements that are relevant to our audit of the financial
statements in Ethiopia, and we have fulfilled our other ethical responsibilities in
or                      accordance with those requirements and the IESBA Code. We believe that the audit
7                evidence we have obtained is sufficient and appropriate to provide a basis for our
opinion.                                              —=

5                       Factory a component included in the Combined Financial Statements. The audit of
“                       ‘Arjo Sugar Factory resulted in an adverse opinion due to material misstatements
related to cash and: bank balance, deficiency in in the control environment in

"1                            Responsibilities of the Factory's Management and the Corporation's Management
oa                                     ‘for the Financial Statements
“                  ‘The Factory's and Project’s Management is responsible for the preparation and fair
EB              presentation of the financial statements in accordance with International Financial
Reporting Standards, and for such intemal control as the Factory's Management
"1                    determines is necessary to enable the preparation of financial statements that are free
                        from material misstatement, whether due to fraud or error.
              The Corporation’s Management is responsible for overseeing the Factory's and
4                  Project's financial reporting process. The Corporation's Management is also
responsible for assessing the Factory’s ability to continue as a going concern, ensuring
                        that disclosures are made, as applicable, of matters related to going concem and using
i                          the going concern basis of accounting unless the Corporation's Management knows of
‘an intention either to liquidate the Factory or to cease operations, or knows that there is
4                         no realistic alternative but to do so.
